data.title: Forvia: Protecting Itself Against Tariff Impact
data.description:
Introduction

Forvia (OTCPK:FURCF) (OTCPK:FAURY) is a French car parts supplier with a worldwide presence and increasing sales into China. The company has been trying to clean up its act after the acquisition of Hella, but as I mentioned in my previous article, the financial improvements are quite slow. That being said, there's a noticeable improvement, and as the company is rapidly covering its exposure to US tariffs, Forvia is still worth a closer look.

Forvia has its primary listing on Euronext Paris, where it's trading with FRVIA as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is approximately 1.6 million shares per day for a monetary value of around 11M EUR per day. I will use the Euro as the base currency throughout this article as it’s the currency the company reports its financial results in and it’s the currency for its most liquid listing.

Somewhat upbeat after Q1 – but it’s not an easy world

In the first quarter of 2025, Forvia reported a total revenue of 6.7B EUR, an increase of approximately 2.6% of which about 80% came from organic growth initiatives with the remainder coming from currency impact changes. Forvia continues to grow its business with Chinese OEMs at a double-digit rate. China now accounts for about 20% of the company’s reported revenue and remains a very important growth pillar for Forvia.

Unfortunately, the company does not provide detailed financial statements on a quarterly basis, and we’ll have to wait for the H1 report to get a better look at the underlying performance and operations.

That’s why I think it makes sense to quickly look back at the FY 2024 results to get a better understanding of the platform Forvia continues to build on.

In 2024, the company reported a total revenue of just under 27B EUR, which was a 1% decrease compared to the preceding year. Total operating income after amortizing acquired intangible assets fell by approximately 3% to 1.21B EUR, and after recording some additional non-recurring expenses, the pre-tax income was just 229M EUR on a reported basis, which resulted in a net loss of 24M EUR. That being said, there was a 161M EUR profit that was attributable to the non-controlling interests, which means the net loss attributable to the Forvia shareholders was roughly 185M EUR.

While that definitely doesn’t sound great, keep in mind there are quite a few non-recurring items that weighed on the reported results with, for instance, a 362M EUR hit from expenses related to the reorganization of the corporate structure and a 84M EUR "other" hit including litigation settlements.

The company also publishes its "net cash flow calculation" and that’s an interesting one. As you can see below, the total net cash flow in FY 2024 was 655M EUR, but this includes 619M EUR in working capital contributions and 208M EUR in cash restructuring expenses.

If I’d filter out those two items, the underlying adjusted net cash flow was approximately 245M EUR or 1.24 EUR per share.

The guidance was reconfirmed despite the impact of tariffs

So despite the uncertainty when it comes to international trade, Forvia has reconfirmed its full-year guidance after a successful start of the year.

The company now expects a total revenue of 26.3-2.7.5B EUR and an operating margin of 5.2-6% of the revenue. Meanwhile, the balance sheet should continue to get stronger as the company now expects to have a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of less than 1.8 by the end of this year, and this should decrease further to 1.5 by the end of net year thanks to the impact of a positive free cash flow, improving margins and selling off some assets.

The company has also provided an update on how it plans and expects to navigate through the current, unpredictable climate. Forvia uses local networks of industrial operations and suppliers in North America, and most of the sales that currently find place between Mexico and the US are already subject to duties. And for the US-based purchases of components outside of the US, according to Forvia, in excess of 80% of those are eligible for USMCA qualification.

Forvia is also already in discussions with its clients to get them to sign pass-through agreements. The company mentioned it has already reached agreements for almost 50% of its residual exposure, and it aims to fully cover its exposure.

Should new tariffs be enacted, Forvia will deploy a similar approach and secure pass-through agreements.

Investment thesis

Forvia’s share price has now been in a pretty continuous decline for about four years now, and although the company is working hard to turn the tide, the market is now a bit wary of suppliers to the automotive industry until it sees how all the tariff talk actually plays out.

Forvia is actually delivering on its expectations: The margins are improving, and the balance sheet is getting stronger. The equity is currently valued at less than 0.5 times the adjusted EBITDA, and the enterprise value right now represents less than 2.5 times the adjusted EBITDA. This makes Forvia cheap on pretty much any metric you look at, and I hope the company will also start to report decent earnings and free cash flow, so the market can start to appreciate Forvia a bit more.

I had written out of the money put options on Forvia before, and those expired out of the money. I have now started to write put options again, with the first batch having exercise prices at 6.2 and 6.4 EUR. I'm trying to write more put options, but I’m in no rush and prefer to get a good price more than a fast fill.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The High Yield Playbook: Positions And Possibilities
data.description:
By Joseph P. Lynch & Rachel Young

Explore how wider spreads, tariff impacts, and sector-specific trends are reshaping the high yield market.

Trnascript

Anu Rajakumar: From the evolving composition of the high-yield market to the impact of macroeconomic factors such as tariff-induced inflation and shifting interest rates, the current credit environment is as dynamic as ever. At the same time, trends like the rising appeal of loans and CLOs, as well as increased interest in European credit, are reshaping how investors approach fixed income strategies. But how can investors navigate this complex environment to uncover value beyond investment grade? What factors are driving these trends, and how should we assess risks and opportunities in today's market?

My name is Anu Rajakumar, and joining me today is Joseph Lynch, global head of non-investment grade credit, and a senior portfolio manager for non-investment grade credit, and Rachel Young, director of non-investment grade credit research and a senior research analyst. Joe, Rachel, welcome to Disruptive Forces.

Joe: Thanks for having me, Anu.

Rachel: Great to be here.

Anu: Joe, let's start with you. The credit market has seen significant shifts recently. Why don't you tell us about the current environment as it relates to non-investment grade credit?

Joe: Sure. The biggest thing is spreads are much wider than where they were at the beginning of the year, which simply means that prices are lower and the risk premium that investors demand over treasuries is greater. That's been driven by a change in investors' outlook for growth. We came into the year expecting fairly strong growth between 2 and 2.5%. With the most recent announcements from the administration around tariffs, our growth expectation and the expectation of growth for the market has come in considerably. That's now somewhere between 0 and 1%. That's really changed the dynamics for the market, and that has started to raise the specter of much higher defaults in the non-IG market.

Anu: Great. That's a very good backdrop. Thank you very much. Those comments certainly complement the publication that your team put out quite recently. That's the quarterly fixed income outlook. In this piece, tariffs, of course, were mentioned along with the expectation for the impact on economic growth and inflation. In that piece, as you know, Joe, the team wrote, "Amid the dislocation, we are finding opportunities for careful credit selection." Joe, tell us a little bit about where you are seeing the most attractive opportunities in credit today.

Joe: There's really very interesting opportunities across all three of the major areas of non-investment grade credit. That would be high-yield bonds, bank loans, and CLO debt. It's just you need to know where to look in those specific asset classes to find value. Why we think those areas are more compelling today is the fact that spreads are much wider than where they were at the beginning of the year. You are being fairly compensated for the volatility and the fundamental risk that exists in the market. As you dig into all three of those asset classes, there's some very compelling and interesting opportunities.

For example, in high yield, the market has changed significantly over the past 10 to 20 years. We think spreads relative to the fundamentals of that market look very attractive. For investors who are thinking that interest rates may stay higher for longer, the bank loan asset class offers attractive carry today that we think is compelling. Then finally, for investors that maybe are a little bit more cautious, the CLO debt market is very interesting in our opinion. Investors can pick and choose what level of risk that they want to take, given the embedded structural protections that exist in the CLO market.

Anu: That's great. Thank you very much. I want to pick up on something that you said in that previous answer, Joe. You mentioned the high-yield market has seen a significant evolution over the past 10 to 20 years. Let's just dig into that a little bit. What are some of those key changes that you've observed?

Joe: Yes, sure. There's really three or four pretty major changes. The first is from a credit quality perspective. Double B’s as a percentage of the overall market is at an all-time high. The duration of the high-yield market is about as short as it's ever been at 3.2 years. The percentage of the market that is secured by assets of the issuers is also at an all-time high, in excess of 40%. Finally, just use of proceeds. The financings that back these transactions have been mostly used for refinancings, as opposed to historically much more for M&A or LBO activities. Just the underlying credit quality of the high yield market is much higher today than what it's been in the past. When investors think about volatility and risk, they look towards what were historical spreads in the high yield market during last bouts of volatility and crises. Investors have typically said between 500 and 600 basis points, that is a attractive entry point for high yield.

The reality is, over the last 10 years, high yield in both the US and Europe has spent very little time wide of 600 basis points.. At 500 basis points, the market has typically generated a double-digit type return over the subsequent two years. Today, given that backdrop that the high yield market is of such higher quality today, we think even inside of 500 basis points is attractive.

Anu: Absolutely. Now, Rachel, let's turn to you and let's hear a little bit more about those opportunities. From a sector-specific perspective, are there any underappreciated sectors or industries where you are seeing unique resilience, particularly amidst this tariff-related set of challenges or other headwinds in the market?

Rachel: Yes. It's a great question and one that I think is particularly interesting in this environment of global macroeconomic uncertainty and the level of risk that we might see today in the current tariff regime. It might surprise individuals to know that the overall market from a high yield and loan perspective, in our opinion, actually has relatively limited direct impact to sectors that we see in really the focus areas of tariffs. We put that in the context of closer to 10% of the high-yield and loan market. With that brings a lot of opportunities.

I think the sectors that we would emphasize, that we see some of those greatest opportunities, would be US-centric businesses.

Services sector I think is a great example of that, in which those issuers tend to be very stable businesses, low exposure to tariffs, and really have great overall durable cash flow profiles. We think the services sector is a great example. Then lastly, we also find issuers across the utility sector to be having limited impact overall to tariffs, but also on the right side of longer-term secular trends that we're seeing in terms of the energy transition across the US, the need for power generation to fuel data center growth. These businesses also have strong cash flow characteristics and benefit from overall physical asset presence that we think provides great collateral coverage.

Anu: Yes, that's great. I'm just curious if you can go a little bit more into your process as a research analyst. How do you actually go about assessing the impact of tariffs on these specific sectors that you highlighted, particularly for companies and industries that are reliant on global supply chains? How do you do that, and how do you manage some of those risks that are inherent in your investment decisions?

Rachel: Yes. It's a great question, and I think it really boils down to strong diligence at the underlying issuer level in terms of the overall exposure a company might have to tariffs. It's important to really have a strong understanding of the underlying supply chain dynamics that a company has in terms of their dependency on foreign imports, for example. We also need to make important assessments around the price elasticity of a company's products to the extent that we do find the issuer to be exposed to tariffs and the overall flexibility a company has to potentially offset those.

Interestingly, we have conducted scenario planning and analysis and stress testing issuers around these various dynamics, and we make an overall assessment in terms of their impact to their credit profiles.

Anu: Absolutely. I'm sure one of those assessments is just on defaults in general. I'm just curious, what is your current thinking on default levels in this space?

Rachel: Our team, just for some background, we conduct a bottom-up assessment of the likelihood of default across all issuers in both the loan and bond index. Importantly, as part of that assessment, we are drilling into a company's exposure to various assumptions. Our latest assumptions included a moderation and overall economic growth similar to what Joe had mentioned earlier in terms of our overall expectations there. In addition, it included this overlay of the current tariff regime.

Given the overall tariff uncertainty, we felt it was important to include that in our assessment. The outcome of that, from a base case perspective, was certainly an increase in our forecasted defaults over a two-year cumulative cycle relative to what we would have seen in the fourth quarter. However, the outcome was still one that would be a characteristic that would be similar to historical default levels from an average basis over time.

Anu: Right. Terrific. Maybe Joe, I'll turn to you now. We talked all about this moderating of economic growth and the trade uncertainty. With all that being said, European credit markets appear to have been drawing more interest from global investors. Joe, what's driving that trend, and how are you positioning to capture some of those opportunities in Europe or other areas around the world?

Joe: Sure. I think there's a couple important drivers of the change in Europe. One would be fundamentals. I think the European Union, particularly Germany, is undertaking potentially pretty significant fiscal investment, and that will drive growth throughout Europe, and we think that will be a strong positive for the region. That's a pretty significant change from what we've seen out of the region over the past decade. The other big change would be technicals. We're seeing a significant interest from investors investing in Europe. This is both European investors keeping capital in their own region, as well as other foreign investors now maybe reallocating some capital away from the US and putting that towards Europe.

I would say Asia is a area of particular interest where you might see more of those capital flows to Europe relative to the US. That's being driven obviously by a number of factors, some of which are related to tariffs and others are related to the potential growth opportunities in both of those markets. Europe has the potential for this virtuous cycle of incremental capital coming into their market, potentially driving down rates, potentially creating more demand, a positive technical backdrop, which will be good for the fundamentals of many of the issuers in that region.

I would say where we have the flexibility, we have been allocating more of our capital recently into European issuers or multinational issuers. In many of our multi-sector portfolios, we have the ability to allocate regionally, and at a high level, we believe the relative value looks much more attractive there. There's just a little bit more certainty around the growth outlook than what we're seeing in the US today.

Anu: Great. That's super helpful. Thank you very much. Now, looking ahead, what would you say are the most important takeaways for investors who are navigating the current credit environment? How should they balance these opportunities that we've been discussing today, along with the risks, to position themselves effectively in today's market? That's a question for both of you to answer.

Joe: Yes, sure. I'll take a start at it. I'd say, most importantly, our view is that a severe downside scenario is quite unlikely. The scenario we find ourselves in or the situation we find ourselves in today has really been self-inflicted, which means that it can be corrected relatively easily. Some of these tariffs and other aspects coming out of the administration can be walked back. I think a severe downside scenario of significant increase in defaults is unlikely. Our base case, again, is just a positive but very modest growth environment.

Taking that left tail risk out of the potential opportunities is really important. I think the other is just to think about the fact that our market is liquid, so we can trade around in the names and issuers, and sectors that we cover. The things that we owned six months ago, we don't own today, or as things change in the market, we can get ahead of that and won't have to deal with potential defaults and names that see significant spread widening. I think that's sometimes a underappreciated fact for these non-IG markets.

Rachel: I would add that I think what's really important in today's market is just the importance placed around bottom-up fundamental research. Fundamentally, we think there's opportunities across the landscape as we highlighted, and not only within issuers that potentially could be exposed to tariffs but really digging in and understanding where the risks lie and the opportunities that are presented ahead.

Anu: Terrific. That is great set of responses. Thank you so much for sharing those today. I can't let you go without a quick bonus question. Of course, markets have been quite chaotic in recent weeks. My question for both of you is, after a busy day of analyzing markets, what is your favorite way to unwind?

Joe: The past month, I've spent most of my time outside of work trying to analyze what the Chicago Bears are going to do with their draft picks.

Anu: Is that relaxing or more stressful?

Joe: It's actually been much more relaxing than the markets. I've been happy with the picks, now that the draft is over.

Anu: That's great. Thank you. Rachel, what about you?

Rachel: I would say my favorite way to unwind is going for a nice, long run and finishing the day with a glass of red wine. [chuckles]

Anu: How many miles are you averaging on a relaxing run?

Rachel: 3 to 5.

Anu: That is very admirable. Very good after a long day. I'm impressed. [chuckles] Thank you both for being here. In today's episode, we explored this evolving landscape of non-investment-grade credit. We talked about the impact of wider spreads, tariffs, and moderating growth across high-yield bonds. We also talked about CLOs and leveraged loans as well.

I want to just summarize again those takeaways that Joe and Rachel mentioned at the end. First of all, there was the base case that Joe mentioned positive but modest growth environment.

Second, the importance of recognizing that these are very liquid markets. Thirdly, Rachel's point about the process here in active management, which is really focused on bottoms up fundamental research, which is important for investors to remember as we're seeking to capture the opportunity set in front of us. Joe and Rachel, thank you again for joining me today.

Joe: Great. Thanks for having us.

Rachel: Thank you.

data.title: The 'Goldilocks' Case For Baxter International
data.description:
For about a decade now, it's been difficult to own Baxter International (NYSE:BAX). In the second half of the 2010s, BAX stock looked too expensive; it closed the decade trading at about 23x earnings, thanks in large part to a thesis that its operating margins would keep expanding at a rapid pace.

During the pandemic, it became quickly apparent that health care stocks wouldn't be the winners initially thought (bear in mind that Baxter shares hit their all-time high on April 2, 2020). In recent years, BAX has looked almost too cheap: a quickly compressed earnings multiple signaled legitimate concerns around execution and market share. BAX basically went from 'hedge fund hotel' to 'value trap' without much of a stop in between:

But there's a case that at the moment BAX stock has found a happy medium. At 12.2 the midpoint of 2025 adjusted EPS guidance, valuation looks attractive. But a key divestiture removes one overhang for the stock, and management is emphatic that a turnaround is underway. That attitude is further buttressed by the fact that Baxter's missteps, including an acquisition that simply has not worked out, are both concrete and in the rearview mirror.

It's certainly possible that management is wrong, and that Baxter structurally and permanently is not the business investors believed it was last decade. But with green shoots in the turnaround, at this price Baxter stock looks worth that risk.

A Weak Reaction To Strong Earnings

BAX stock is actually down 1.4% since the day before its first quarter earnings release on the morning of May 1 — which seems strange. There's a case that the market (which did bid up shares yesterday) is way late in reacting here, and that BAX very well could be up double-digits since the print.

From a headline perspective, the quarter looks solid. Adjusted earnings per share of $0.55 was 6.5 cents better than the midpoint of guidance ($0.47-$0.50). The full-year outlook didn't move much (the low end came up $0.02), in part because the Q1 strength came from the timing of distributors building inventory, as chief operating officer Heather Knight noted on the earnings call.

But the fact that guidance barely moved certainly isn't bad news, particularly in the context of how BAX has traded over the past five weeks. Shares entered the print down 7.4% from their close before "Liberation Day", when President Donald Trump announced reciprocal tariffs on nearly every country in the world. Yet Baxter seemed to signal that the duties would be relatively immaterial: it's guiding for a $60-$70 million impact this week, mostly in the second half. Notably, that figure assumes the current 90-day 'pause' is ended, with tariffs returning to the levels proposed in April.

The fact that the earnings outlook can incorporate that hit without moving itself should be good news. That said, the hit simply isn't that big: the outlook suggests a hit to EPS of about $0.10 this year, and maybe $0.15 next year. Baxter's figures do include some mitigation of tariff impacts, but there's plenty of time for further improvements assuming the tariffs stay in place (which is far from guaranteed).

In other words, there seems to be room for the decline of the past five weeks to reverse at least — which would suggest an ~8% rally. Add to that the fact that the business posted a strong quarter, and evidence of an early bottom for a struggling business, and, again, there's a case that BAX should be above the $34 level at which it closed March — 10% (at least) better than the $31.17 close the day before earnings.

Green Shoots Of A Turnaround?

The 65% decline in Baxter stock over the past five-plus years has been driven by several factors. The 2021 acquisition of Hill-Rom has been a big problem: the $12.4 billion deal simply hasn't worked out (and levered Baxter up right into a rising interest rate environment). 2022 inflation hurt; Baxter's contracts with GPOs (group purchasing organizations) included some escalators for inflation, but not enough to protect margins. Execution over the period hasn't been spectacular, either: perhaps part of the muted response to Q1 2025 results is based on the fact that the year-prior comparison was soft, because of "operational issues" in the Healthcare Systems & Technology segment (itself a legacy of the Hill-Rom deal. Those issues, which helped drive a 9% decline in segment revenue for the quarter, are not unusual: Baxter simply has not performed well over the past few years.

But if you listen to management after Q1, and in events leading up to the release, there's a repeated refrain that the company is starting to find its footing. Interim chief executive officer Brent Shafer said on the Q1 call that "we are...confident in our strategic trajectory." In the Q&A of the call, Knight — somewhat audaciously — said that "HST, we feel like, is back". Care and Connectivity Solutions, part of HST, is taking share, and Front Line Care (the remainder of the business) performance has stabilized. At a conference in March, chief financial officer Joel Grade pointed to a number of new product launches — among the Novum infusion platform, which is performing well out of the gate — as a sign of improvement after supply chain challenges limited the company earlier this decade.

There is a sense from management that, particularly with the divestiture of the kidney care business to private equity firm Carlyle Group (CG), this is something of a 'new' Baxter. And that sense is not just puffery from executives. The portfolio logic of the Hill-Rom deal still holds, even if the deal was an overpay. The kidney care sale improves the balance sheet: Baxter now is targeting a 3x net leverage (debt to EBITDA) ratio by year-end, a ratio much more appropriate for its business. Revenue guidance for this year was raised to 4-5% growth, a rate management believes Baxter can generate consistently going forward.

A Strong Business At A Good Price

If HST (28% of 2024 revenue) is "back", this does become a pretty good business. It's likely not quite as good as investors believed in the late 2010s, but operating margins are still ~16% with plans for expansion going forward. GPO negotiations already are helping pricing; another is on deck in 2026. Revenues from Carlyle under a TSA (transition services agreement) and an MSA (manufacturing and supply agreement) are helping current numbers, but also are offset by "stranded costs" of about $240 million. The net effect seems to be modestly negative, but by 2027 those costs should be gone, offsetting the loss of TSA/MSA contributions from Carlyle.

The kidney care divestiture helps return on capital, and takes away a notable headwind against the stock (fear that GLP-1s will significantly reduce the need for dialysis). What's left is a good business with solid return on invested capital, potentially high-teens margins, a reasonable growth rate and a defensive posture.

A ~12x price-to-earnings multiple seems too low for that kind of profile. And so it does seem like there's room for both operating margins and multiples to expand. Model 6%-8% annual EPS growth and move the multiple to 15-16x (assuming the market gets more confident in the turnaround) and BAX gets just above $50 over four years (7% annual EPS growth, 15.5x terminal multiple). That's mid-teens annualized returns including the dividend — and if the turnaround is real, that is not a particularly aggressive model.

What Goes Wrong For BAX Stock

Of course, the core risk to BAX is that the turnaround is not real. There have been false starts along the way for both the stock and the business. Management is optimistic, and the arrival of a permanent CEO ostensibly suggests more room for improvement, but management teams are pretty much always optimistic.

There are also the broader sector/industry concerns. BAX 'feels' cheap at this level, particularly relative to how the stock was treated when its business was roaring. But in the context of similar stocks, valuation is not that far out of line. Becton, Dickinson (BDX) is at 11.2x forward earnings, Hologic (HOLX) at 12.3x, and Solventum (SOLV) is under 11x. Over in the device space, GE HealthCare (GEHC), Medtronic (MDT), and Zimmer Biomet (ZBH) aren't getting much more in the way of multiples. Overall, with only a few exceptions the performance of health care stocks since the peak of the pandemic has been exceptionally disappointing:

No doubt that history is weighing on BAX at the moment. But the last few years have been far from normal, with lingering impacts from the pandemic (as well as concerns around pressure on providers, which in turn limits procedure growth and revenue for suppliers). And the sector's valuation gap is literally at historic levels:

There's room for the sector to bounce back. There's more room for Baxter to bounce back. There's more than enough here to take on these risks.

data.title: A Potential Fed Rate Cut Isn't That Significant Now
data.description:
My previous take on the Federal Open Market Committee [FOMC] meeting was titled "The Fed's Job Just Got Tougher," which alluded to maintaining the need for maintaining a balancing act at a time when inflation was forecast to be higher than initially anticipated while growth projections were lowered.

Even as the Fed kept interest rates unchanged at the end of the two-day FOMC meeting that concluded yesterday, the reserve only underlined the development in its latest statement. Here, I look more closely at it and assess what it can mean for monetary policy and the stock markets going forward.

Concern About Growth in Focus

The FOMC statement's focus on growth is most evident. This reflects as follows:

  • Acknowledging the drag on growth

It starts out by acknowledging the recent drag on economic growth, saying "Although swings in net exports have affected the data...". But following this, it remains consistent with the March statement, where it mentioned that:

Recent indicators suggest that economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace.

In the first quarter (Q1 2025), the US economy contracted by 0.3% on an annualized basis following a massive surge in imports (see chart below) as there was a rush to secure purchases before the increased tariffs come into force. The pace of real final sales, which is consumer spending plus gross private fixed investment, however maintained a steady pace of 3% compared to 2.9% in Q4 2025.

  • Further uncertainty highlighted

That said, the Fed has clear concerns about the future growth of the economy. This is evident in two remarks. First, it says, "Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further." "Further" is an addition here to the otherwise exact same remark in March. It bears mentioning here that the Reserve had reduced its median GDP growth forecast for 2025 to a sub-trend 1.7% at that time. The emphasis on economic uncertainty indicates that the expected slowing down can be even bigger now.

Second, it now says, "The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate and judges that the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen", while it ended the remark after "...its dual mandate" in the previous statement. The note on unemployment likely follows from its observation on risks to growth, as the labor market so far looks alright. The risks to inflation are well understood, though.

Small Uptick in Rate Cut Probability

With this as the background, it's hardly surprising that there's a slight uptick in the probability of a 25 basis points [bp] rate cut at the June meeting. As the chart below shows, the likelihood of a reduction in interest rate, as measured by the 30-day Fed Funds futures, is now at 20.2%. This is up from the 17.4% after the last meeting.

At the same time, the overwhelming probability is still accorded to unchanged interest rates. The probability could be more likely to fluctuate until then, than usual though. And this is because the Fed has highlighted its cautious stance on the evolving macroeconomic stance further.

What to Look Out For

Specifically, no less than four data points until the next meeting can be critical in determining the Fed's next move. These are as follows:

  • CPI Inflation: Inflation prints for both April and May are expected in the interim, on May 13 and June 11 respectively. As per the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland's 'Nowcasts', headline CPI inflation is slated to remain relatively subdued, and there's nothing alarming expected for the core CPI inflation figures either. Similarly, for the Fed's preferred inflation measure, the PCE.
  • GDP, Second estimate: If the inflation forecasts are on point, it follows that the Fed's focus will remain firmly on growth. To this extent, the second GDP estimate for Q1 2025 will be important when it releases on May 29. This is particularly so as the actual advance estimate was in contrast with the consensus expectation of a 0.2% increase in the quarter.
  • Labor market report: Finally, the monthly jobs report expected on June 6 will be significant too, especially as the Fed has highlighted the risks to unemployment rate in the latest statement. This follows the Fed already upping its projections for the figure in 2025 by 0.1 percentage points in the March meeting to 4.4%.

Stock Market Response

The stock markets barely responded to the FOMC statement, with all the key indices up by sub-1% at yesterday's trading close. The Dow Jones Industrial Index (DJI) (NYSEARCA:DIA) was up the most of these, by 0.69%, followed by the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPX) (NYSEARCA:SPY), which rose by 0.42% and the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (NASDAQ:QQQ) came next with an increase of 0.39%.

This is telling, considering that yesterday's statement actually indicates that another cut can be in the offing. But for now, clearly, all participants in the macroeconomy have bigger concerns. Namely, tariffs and their implications. This is evident in the fact that year-to-date [YTD], all key indices have still shown a contraction ranging between 3% and 6%.

Sectoral Equities Performance

Further, while the fortunes of key equity sectors have altered significantly since the last FOMC meeting, the overarching theme remains unchanged. It's the time for defensives as market participants continue to expect macroeconomic uncertainty, in tandem with the Fed.

In this vein, the biggest gainer YTD is utilities, coming out ahead of both energy and healthcare (as was the case in March), which have both slipped into the red. That consumer staples is the second biggest gainer among all sectors is also worth highlighting, since it's yet another reflection of investors' tendencies towards safer stocks right now.

Finally, the fact that the consumer discretionary sector remains firmly the worst affected is yet another sign that right now, textbook investing expected during macroeconomic weakness is at play.

In Conclusion

Textbook investing styles are there for a reason, though. They can work. Especially at times like the present, where the ongoing unexpected events are sparking off unpredictability for the future more than an actual economic slowdown.

The slowdown could come in, though, going by the Fed's increased cautiousness about economic growth, even as it stays alert to the likelihood of inflationary pressures. That the probability of a rate cut has inched up as a result, was to be expected.

While so far, it appears unlikely that the Fed will cut rates immediately at the next meeting, there are plenty of data releases in the interim that will suggest more accurately what to expect, including inflation, GDP and jobs market numbers. The key point, though remains that rate cuts are no longer top of mind for investors.

data.title: May BoE Decision: Progress On Inflation Allows Another BoE Cut
data.description:
The Bank of England ("BoE") moved forward in a contested decision with a quarter-point rate cut following its May meeting. There were supporters who were both more hawkish and more dovish in the deliberations, and ultimately, the more neutral position won out. The division is a good representation of the Bank of England walking a tightrope, trying to balance the hotter inflation outlook and cooler growth expectations.

Decision

The Bank of England voted by a narrow 5–4 margin to lower its Bank Rate by 25 bps to 4.25% at its May meeting, marking the start of a gradual policy easing cycle. The decision was highly contested: two members pushed for a larger 50 bp cut, arguing that persistent disinflation and weak domestic demand justified more accommodation, while two others voted to hold rates steady, citing continued inflationary pressures and firming household inflation expectations. The majority ultimately favored a middle ground, judging that inflation progress had been sufficient to begin withdrawing some policy restraint without abandoning a restrictive stance altogether.

The decision reflects growing concern about weakening global growth and uncertainty surrounding new trade policies, as well as slowing domestic momentum. The Bank still expects headline inflation to temporarily rise in Q3 due to energy-related base effects, but sees that increase as transitory. Importantly, the MPC emphasized that monetary policy is not on a preset course and remains data-dependent. However, for those observing the trend, the cut today continues a pattern of alternating between a pause and a 25 bps cut that has been evident since August 2024.

Monetary Policy Report

The May policy announcement comes with an update to the Monetary Policy Report put together by the BoE members, which features key forecast updates and discussion about the economic and monetary policy outlooks. The key parts of the report are updates to the inflation and growth forecasts, which reflect some shifts in data since the last Monetary Policy Report (MPR).

The BoE updated its forecast for inflation after price growth in the first quarter of the year was reported in line with expectations. The estimates for inflation in the next four quarters were raised in the May update, only slightly above the February estimates, but in the medium- to long-term, the BoE actually sees significant disinflation. It now projects CPI inflation (ex-energy) to fall to 2.0% by Q2 2027, while the February forecast did not reach that level. The BoE maintains its position that the increase in inflation in the near term is transitory and that inflationary pressures would dissipate due to “a period of excess economic slack” or weaker growth.

The BoE also updated its forecast for GDP growth, and it reflects some of the upside surprises that we have seen in the monthly data from the ONS. The February forecast for four-quarter GDP growth was well beaten at 1.2% vs the expected 0.4%. Because of that, the near-term estimates of GDP growth have been moved up to reflect a smoother path of real GDP growth over the next few quarters as the BoE still sees economic growth weakening in the short term. This partly incorporates expectations that the volatile trade environment will cause excess supply to ”widen over the next couple of years, to just under 1% of potential GDP, before narrowing by the end of the forecast period.”

The May edition of the Monetary Policy Report made clear that the primary reason for today’s rate cut was the ongoing “progress in reducing domestic inflationary pressures,” which gave the Bank enough confidence to begin gradually withdrawing policy restraint. At the same time, the BoE flagged growing global trade uncertainty, particularly around U.S. tariffs, as an additional downside risk to UK growth and a counterweight to any potential upside inflation risks. This caution comes despite stronger-than-expected GDP growth in late 2024 and early 2025.

Still, the Bank did not commit to any future path of policy, noting that monetary policy would need to stay restrictive “until the risks to inflation returning sustainably to the 2% target in the medium term have dissipated further,” signaling a continued emphasis on a data-dependent approach.

Market Reaction

The market reacted quite hawkishly to the Bank of England’s quarter-point cut as bond yields jumped immediately after the move was announced, and the pound appreciated against both the U.S. dollar (GBP:USD) and the euro. The moves in the 2-year and the 10-year gilt markets were especially sharp as investors sent rates up about 10 bps and 6 bps, respectively, immediately after the announcement. Similarly, the GBPUSD jumped from around 1.325 to around 1.332 about an hour following the release of the statement. The FTSE 100 developed a more subtle trend, but in the end, the index dropped about -0.3% from the highs of the day.

It appears the market thought the Bank of England would be more dovish, or at the very least, would be more agreeable on the decision of a quarter-point cut. The dissent evident in the decision suggests that the BoE is far from moving towards a more fully accommodative monetary policy path to support growth, as inflation is still a heavy focus in decision-making. With that being said, I believe that, if conditions remain roughly in line with the outlook, the BoE will continue with the cadence that it has established since August 2024 of alternating between pauses and cuts. This appears to be a comfortable, gradual pace of easing restrictive monetary policy that the BoE likes and will maintain until data suggests the need for a change.

data.title: ONEOK Has Good Potential But Selling Pressures Continue (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
After reaching a peak of $118 at the end of 2024, OKE was falling steadily and facing selling pressure again at every pause. Finally, we're at $79 right now. Even though earnings reports have not always met expectations, this doesn't mean that the company is not profitable or has a bright future.

In this article, I will explain why OKE’s financials, valuations, and various quantitative data point to the stock being on the optimistic side despite its continued decline and why I am a “hold” for the stock.

Financials

When I look at the company's income, I don't see a bad picture. Even though the income tends to decline until 2023, income that stabilized after 2023 was on a sharp rise since the last quarter of 2024.

The income started to increase YoY in the 4th quarter of 2023 and continues to grow without slowing down. When the company's last 3-year 2nd quarter growth average is taken, income expectation is $7.92 billion; when the last 3-year 2nd quarter growth average is taken, income expectation is calculated as $7.67.

So my income expectation for the next quarter is in the range of $7.92-7.67. Due to the increase in the last quarter and especially the clear uptrend in EBITDA after 2023, I think that the income will be around $7.92. This means that the company's income will grow by 61.79% YoY, which is a very positive view.

I determined that the net margin was at a median of 11.69% in the last 3 years. So my net income expectation is in the range of $925-897 million. Based on this data, I have to say that my EPS expectation is an average of $1.48.

According to Seeking Alpha data, since the market's EPS expectation for the next quarter is $1.33, I see the probability of an EPS announcement above market expectations as high in light of this quantitative data and analysis.

DuPont Analysis

I want to apply DuPont analysis to OKE, which allows me to clearly see which variable has an impact on the RoE by breaking it down.

I see a decline in the company's financial leverage and asset turnover from 2021 to 2023, but this decline seems to have stopped later, and the data seems to have stabilized again. There was an uptrend in asset turnover since the second half of 2024. Despite margin pressures, the company's intermediate assets (pipelines, operating facilities) continue to maintain their productivity.

Net margin is entering a serious volatility in late 2022 and early 2023. Even though it is on an uptrend after 2023, it has been falling especially since mid-2024. So I see that profitability per revenue has decreased. I think the reasons for this are due to higher operating costs or acquisitions such as Magellan/EnLink.

The biggest share in the decline in RoE seems to belong to net margin. Since the leverage, which is the equity multiplier, is fixed and stable, there is no problem in the company's capital structure. In general, there is a negativity related to operations.

So, even though the decline in RoE creates a negative impression, determining that the problem is not structural means that the solutions to the problems are relatively easy.

Economic Profit

The company's financial profitability is in a very good state, but it is also important to look at economic profitability. Economic profitability, unlike financial or accounting profit, is an analysis method that analyzes whether the stock is growing profitably using WACC and ROIC data. My observations and experiences show that a stock with positive and stable economic profitability generally tends to rise in the long term.

When I look at OKE's economic profitability since 2021, I see that it was above 5% until 2023, but it has been below 5% since 2023. I can honestly say that it is stable, and its volatility is also very low. There is no specific uptrend, but when I analyze economic profitability, I usually don't look for an uptrend as long as there is stability. Even though OKE's economic profitability is not high, I can say that it gives an impression of being positive as long as it remains above 0%.

Forward P/E

The company's financial profitability is moving towards positive. Even though the company is not perfect economically, it is in a position that is shifting from neutral to positive. At least there is stability, and no structural problems are evident. So what do the company's valuations say? Is OKE stock cheap or expensive to buy? To find out, I first want to do a forward P/E analysis.

When I look at OKE's forward P/E since 2016, I see a clear downtrend. While the company's forward P/E was almost 30x in 2017, it is now far from these extremes and is at more reasonable levels.

Even though it seems to be in a cheap position when looking at its peak in 2017, I see that OKE's forward P/E has seen a maximum of 17.89x and a minimum of 11.52x since 2021. So, the average is at 14.98x.

When I consider that OKE's current forward P/E is 14.39x, I think the company is at an average level. In other words, historically, the company is very well priced according to forward P/E.

When we consider that the general forward P/E of the SP500, the index in which the stock is included, is 20.91x, so the company is very cheap compared to the index. In addition to that, when I take the average of forward P/Es of OKE and its competitors in the same sector, EPD, WMB, ET, KMI, MPLX, TRGP, and LNG, I reach 17.32x. OKE also has a very good forward P/E ratio compared to its competitors in the sector, and it is in a cheaper position.

As a result of that, while OKE historically appears in its price in forward P/E valuation, it is a much cheaper stock compared to the index and its competitors in the sector. That's why I can say that it is positioned on the cheap but still kind of neutral side.

Price Performance and Charting

Before we get into price performance, I would like to clarify a detail that may confuse many investors. OKE has generally been in direct correlation with Brent oil since 2010. This correlation turned negative between 2011 and 2015, and OKE's price increased significantly while Brent oil stays in a parallel channel. The same situation occurred again, and OKE diverged from Brent again from 2023 to early 2025.

The most important reason for this divergence is OKE's fee-based income model (which accounts for 90-95% of earnings). In addition to that, the stock's exposure to natural gas instead of crude oil also supported this divergence. While oil prices fell due to OPEC's production increases and tariffs, OKE increased its volume by strengthening its infrastructure with strategic acquisitions such as Magellan and EnLink.

OKE's advantage here is that it is an intermediary company and its earnings are based on volumes transported/processed, not market prices. That's why it has serious insulation against direct commodity price fluctuations. So, analyzing OKE by looking only at commodity prices is unfortunately not effective enough. Since I have seen investors who set up such investment strategies, I wanted to point this out and prevent wrong decisions to some extent.

Now that I have clarified this issue, I can directly move on to price analysis. OKE has continued to hold on to the Hodrick-Prescott filter since 2021. OKE started the hard bull run after 2024 by using this filter as support once in 2021, twice in 2022, and twice in 2023.

We see that OKE, which saw $118 in the last quarter of 2024, is currently at $79.75. OKE, which was falling since the beginning of 2025, finally reached the HP filter I mentioned, and I expect it to find support here. The HP filter is exactly at $75, and this area is also a price action area that OKE has used as both support and resistance since 2022.

Especially at the beginning of 2022, 2023, and 2024, OKE was rejected from $72 many times, showing us that the $72-75 area is a clear resistance. We saw that OKE, which broke this resistance at the beginning of the run in 2024, retested $75 in 2024 and confirmed this area as support. Also, in 2025, OKE tested $75 as support in early April.

I currently think that the company will test this price action and HP support level again and try to hold on to this area. But the problem is that when I look at the MACD, I see that the negative momentum is still not over, and OKE is facing selling pressure again and again. This is not good news.

While I think it is unlikely that OKE will fall below $75, given the valuations position the stock on the cheap side and I expect it to face buying pressure at these levels, I don't see enough positive momentum for an upside.

Risks

First of all, this article covers only the technical and quantitative side of the stock. That's why I don't recommend investing without analyzing the articles of my other writer friends, who analyze qualitative data.

Despite OKE's great progress, I think it is necessary to remember that EnLink and Medallion integrations carry implementation risks. The possibility of various delays or cost overruns in the synergies expected to be $250 million in 2025 should definitely be taken into account. In addition to that, the loss of profit from the 3 natural gas pipelines sold to DT Midstream will definitely reduce cash flow visibility.

It is also necessary to pay attention to weather conditions. The decrease in ethane recovery in the mid-continent region may continue the decline in NGL volumes. Even though it is not a long-term risk, I think it may create problems in the short term.

Conclusion

OKE, which is in a neutral position with a positive financial tendency, continues to decline in the short term due to the net margin. While its economic profitability is positive and stable, valuation results also show that the stock is positioned on the cheap side. Despite this, the stock's price action and charting analysis indicate the continuation of negative momentum. That's why, even though I think OKE has the potential to improve itself in the long term, I currently find it appropriate to stay on the "hold" side for the stock due to negative pressures.

data.title: VIS: An Opportunity To Gain From The Manufacturing Slump To The Next Rebound
data.description:
Introduction

Manufacturing activity in the U.S. has been weak for over two years after a rapid expansion witnessed in the post-pandemic rebound.

Of course, manufacturing activity shows cyclicality, and we are used to seeing cycles of expansion followed by recessions. This time, however, it strikes us particularly because both the Biden and the Trump administrations have underlined in different ways how it is strategic for the U.S. to reshore manufacturing plants. So, even though the government pours billions into combating inflation and fuel manufacturing (both through public spending and fiscal or trade policy), we are still not seeing signs of a convincing rebound. Part of this may be explained through the pull-forward effect that would make us consider the post-pandemic surge as a phenomenon that anticipated a lot of demand for goods that would have been spread over a longer timeframe.

At the same time, we are seeing a slow recovery from the 2010 lows of U.S. manufacturing employment, as shown by this chart available on Seeking Alpha.

Now, there are reasons to expect a manufacturing activity rebound, both because of the long recession we are going through and because of government spending.

In my case, I have hand-picked a few of the best industrials and have particularly focused on machinery manufacturers such as Deere (DE) or Caterpillar (CAT). I have been a shareholder of the former but sold it last year on an alluring after-earnings price pop. Recently, I shared a comparison between Caterpillar and Deere to understand which one could be the best pick amidst a tariff-determined environment.

I am also exposed to the ground transportation sector, working on Class 1 railroads such as Norfolk Southern (NSC) and Canadian National (CNI). Perhaps the latest and most interesting addition to my portfolio was United Rentals (URI). It is the leading equipment rental company in the U.S., and it runs a business that is both protected from tariffs and exposed to the tailwind of rising second-hand equipment prices (URI sells its equipment after a few years). Although I stink at market timing, my mid-April buy has turned out to be quite good, and the stock is up 10%.

Now, from the YtD heat map, my picks among industrials are not the sector-leading ones: railroads are down by a few percentage points, and rentals are mixed. Farm and heavy construction is seeing strength in farming equipment, while companies such as Caterpillar (CAT) and Paccar (PCAR) are coming down from their peaks (perhaps new opportunities are opening up). On the other hand, aerospace and defense are mostly in the green, driving the sector up.

Now, there might be some who prefer to invest in the whole sector without cherry-picking a few stocks. In this case, several ETFs track the industry in different ways the industry.

I recently reviewed the Industrial Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA: XLI), but there is another option, which is the Vanguard Industrials Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VIS). The latter has 367 holdings, while XLI only has 78. Moreover, XLI is more concentrated in its top holdings, while VIS spreads the weights of its stocks more evenly.

For example, XLI sees aerospace and defense with a 24.5% weight, while VIS shows a 20.5% weight.

Their top ten holdings are the same, even though the weights are different. For example, XLI has Uber (UBER) as its second-largest holding, while in VIS's case, we have it as number 5 with a 2.66% weight.

Stock XLI Weight VIS Weight
GE Aerospace 5.42% 4.17%
Uber Technologies 4.31% 2.66%
RTX Corp 4.14% 3.39%
Caterpillar Inc 3.73% 3.06%
Boeing Co 3.38% 2.07%
Honeywell Intl. 3.37% 2.65%
Union Pacific Corp 3.16% 2.75%
Automatic Data Processing 2.99% 2.39%
Deere & Co 2.95% 2.34%
Eaton Corp PLC 2.85% 2.07%

When we consider the dividend yield, VIS offers a 1.3% while XLI is a bit higher with 1.44%. At the same time, the 5-yr dividend growth CAGR is higher for VIS: 5.04% vs. 4.44%.

VIS also has lower liquidity, with an AUM of $5.34B (XLI: $19.36B) and an average daily share volume that is around 120k (XLI: 11M). As a result, the average daily dollar volume for VIS is around $30M, while XLI is close to $1.5B.

Like every Vanguard fund, its expense ratio is extremely competitive at 0.09%. Similar ETFs have way higher costs.

As we can see from the ETF grades Seeking Alpha awards, the two ETFs are more or less balanced: VIS has lower risk but also lower liquidity and a slightly weaker momentum; XLI does better in expenses and liquidity.

I think the choice between the two comes from how each one of us views the industry and wants to gain exposure to it, considering the tailwinds that many are waiting to appear. For those who believe that aerospace and defense will have the lion's share of the growth, then XLI is surely a better pick. It also has the nice addition of having Uber as the second-largest holding, offering exposure to a capital-light business.

Those who believe that the whole industry will benefit from government spending and reshoring activity might choose VIS, giving preference also to a slightly faster dividend growth pace.

Therefore, I consider both VIS and XLI interesting buys, with the addition that VIS could soon test its ATHs and perhaps offer a breakout if manufacturing activity rebounds strongly in the next few quarters.

data.title: Lucid: Another Revenue Miss
data.description:
After the bell on Tuesday, we received first quarter results from Lucid (NASDAQ:LCID). The luxury electric vehicle maker has been one of the most disappointing companies in the space in recent years, failing to meet its long-term growth targets. While the company reiterated its full-year production guidance this week, the financials here remain extremely ugly, keeping shares close to their all-time public trading low.

Previous Coverage of the Name

When I last looked at Lucid back in late February, the company announced its fourth quarter results, along with the surprise resignation of co-founder Peter Rawlinson. At that time, revenues beat significantly reduced street estimates, but large losses and cash burn continued. As the company was early in the midst of its Gravity SUV production ramp, investors also had to digest the news of a new interim leader.

My sell rating on this stock has been in place since shares were over $18 back in the summer of 2022, and the stock has lost almost all of its value since then. Lucid shares have actually risen a little since my previous report, a gain of more than 2%, outpacing the S&P's loss of about 4%, but it is a drop in the bucket compared to the losses investors have seen over time.

The Q1 2025 Report

For the quarter, Lucid reported revenues of $235 million. While this number was up 36% over the prior year period, it did miss street estimates by more than $11 million. This ended a streak of three consecutive top line beats, after Lucid had mostly been known for missing street revenue expectations. Lucid delivered over 3,100 vehicles in the quarter, a more than 58% increase as compared to Q1 2024.

The main problem for Lucid is that its low volumes have led to dramatic operating losses. Gross margins in the period were negative 97%, and while this was an improvement of about 37 percentage points over the prior year period, that is definitely not a sustainable business model. Operations lost over $691 million in the quarter, nearly three times the amount of revenue generated. Investors are hoping that as the Gravity ramp leads to more delivery records, losses will decline, but Lucid is not projected to see any kind of profitability for some time.

These large losses have led to significant cash burn over time. Lucid burned through about $590 million in Q1, although this was an improvement from the $715 million figure seen a year earlier. The company ended Q1 with a little more than $4.5 billion in cash and investments against about $2 billion in total debt. Lucid ended the quarter with approximately $5.76 billion in total liquidity, but it may look to raise additional funds to help with the Gravity production ramp as well as the launch of a more affordable vehicle in 2026.

Lucid finished Q1 with about 3.05 billion shares outstanding, up significantly from the more than 1.83 billion figure seen just two years earlier. We've seen numerous debt and equity offerings to keep the name afloat in recent years, with a lot of help coming from the Saudi Public Investment Fund ("PIF"). The PIF currently owns a majority of Lucid shares.

2025 Growth Expectations

When Lucid went public through a special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC") back in 2021, there were hopes for tremendous growth. At that time, management thought it would produce 20,000 Air Sedans in 2022, and by 2025, production of at least three vehicles (including the Gravity) would total 135,000 units. As we've seen with most electric vehicle startups, dramatic growth has been hard to come by. The Gravity was eventually delayed, and Lucid's third model is now a 2026 story.

After producing just over 9,000 vehicles last year, the company originally called for 20,000 units of production this year. With trade war concerns sparking fears of global economic slowdowns, there were fears that this forecast would come down, but management did reiterate it on Tuesday. However, revenue estimates have still dropped to a new low, as seen below, as analysts do remain at least a little skeptical.

I will be curious to see as this year progresses if Lucid breaks down its production and or delivery figures by model. This would allow us to see how the Gravity ramp is going, but also if Lucid is able to grow unit sales of the Air Sedan in a material way. Like many EVs out there, the Air has seen its share of promotions to boost sales volumes, and further incentives would provide an added headwind to the margin progress story.

A Look at Current Valuation

While Lucid is expected to show some strong revenue percentage growth in the coming years, it also remains a very expensive stock currently. As the chart below shows, only Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has a price-to-sales ratio, based on current respective 2025 analyst estimates, that is higher than Lucid. The other names in this space I've used as a comparison are VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO Inc. (NIO), and Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY). These numbers were as of Wednesday's close.

If you average the 7 names other than Lucid, the result is about 2.67 times each's expected 2025 revenue. Lucid finished Wednesday going for about 5.10 times, a significant premium to this comparison group. If we were to exclude Tesla, however, that average drops to just 1.61 times, and these numbers don't include traditional US automakers like Ford Motor Company (F) or General Motors Company (GM) that trade at about 0.25 times their expected revenues for this year. The average Lucid price target as of Wednesday night was $2.53, implying low double-digit upside, but that number had been dramatically cut from almost $37 just three years ago.

There remain two potential major risks for those betting against the name. The first one is that the Saudis just take this company private, buying out the rest of Lucid that they don't already own. The second risk here is that there's a meaningful improvement in the company's financial results in 2025, once the Gravity really starts to see meaningful deliveries, sparking some enthusiasm for the mid-size platform expected to arrive in 2026.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

In the end, Lucid announced mixed results on Tuesday. The company did make some progress on the margin front, but it did miss revenue estimates, and it is still losing tons of money. With cash burn likely to continue for some time, another capital raise is not out of the question, and the valuation remains quite a bit inflated compared to many peers in the space.

While this should be a strong year for delivery and revenue growth in percentage terms, Lucid remains a sell in my opinion. The luxury electric vehicle space is very small, and Lucid hasn't shown that it can hit its growth targets or meaningfully improve its financials. Perhaps the mid-size platform launch will get me to eventually change my rating, but that's still about a year out, and Lucid has never proven it can produce anything in sizable volumes to this point. Should management eventually cut yearly guidance due to global economic pressures, a new low for shares would not surprise me.
